manifest.name: Button
manifest.description: The Button component is a fundamental UI element designed for user interaction, enabling a wide range of actions from submitting forms to navigating between pages. It supports several visual `variants` (default, destructive, outline, secondary, ghost, link) and `sizes` (default, sm, lg, icon) to fit diverse design needs. A key feature is the `loading` prop, which displays a visual indicator and disables the button during asynchronous operations, providing clear user feedback. Furthermore, the `asChild` prop, powered by `@radix-ui/react-slot`, offers exceptional flexibility by allowing the button to render as any child element (e.g., an `<a>` tag for navigation) while retaining all its styles and functionalities. This makes it a highly adaptable component for various interactive scenarios.

valueDescriptions.default: Primary button for general actions and positive confirmations.
valueDescriptions.destructive: Red-themed button for dangerous or irreversible actions like deletion.
valueDescriptions.outline: Button with a visible border, suitable for secondary actions or less emphasized interactions.
valueDescriptions.secondary: Subtle button with a distinct background, for less prominent actions.
valueDescriptions.ghost: Transparent button with minimal visual impact, often used for tertiary actions or within toolbars.
valueDescriptions.link: Button styled as a text link, retaining button semantics but appearing as inline text.

valueDescriptions.default: Standard size button for most common use cases.
valueDescriptions.sm: Small size button, useful for compact interfaces or less critical actions.
valueDescriptions.lg: Large size button, for primary calls to action or increased prominence.
valueDescriptions.icon: Square button designed to exclusively contain an icon, often used for toolbar actions or compact controls.

behaviors.name: loading
behaviors.description:
Shows a loading indicator and disables the button.
When loading, the button is non-interactive and shows aria-busy for accessibility.
@example ```tsx
<Button loading>Submitting...</Button>
```

other.name: asChild
other.description: When true, the button will render as its child element (using Radix Slot). Useful for rendering as a link or custom element while keeping button styles. @example ```tsx ```

guidance.whenToUse: Use when an interactive element is needed to trigger an action, submit a form, or navigate to a new page. Ideal for primary, secondary, and destructive actions within an application. Leverage the `asChild` prop to style links or other elements as buttons.
guidance.whenNotToUse: Do not use for purely decorative elements or static text. If an element's primary purpose is just display rather than interaction, consider a simple link, paragraph, or div. Avoid overusing many different button variants on a single screen, which can confuse users.
guidance.accessibility: The button automatically handles `aria-busy` when in a `loading` state, indicating its non-interactive status to assistive technologies. When using the `asChild` prop, ensure the underlying child element (e.g., `<a>`, `<Link>`) provides its own appropriate accessibility attributes (e.g., `href`, `role`, `aria-label`) to maintain semantic correctness. Standard keyboard navigation (Tab, Enter, Space) and click interactions are fully supported.
